The wine list is a deep dive into Central Otago and wider NZ. The tree-filled courtyard is one of Queenstown's most pleasant dining spaces.

reviews.summary: Consistently ranked among Queenstown's best restaurants. The heritage building and courtyard impress. Some find the celebrity-chef prices high, but the quality is reliable.

pois.name: Fergburger
pois.short: New Zealand's most famous burger joint — the queue is part of the Queenstown experience.
pois.commentary: Fergburger has been Queenstown's most iconic food institution since 2001. The burgers are enormous, generously loaded and made with quality ingredients — the Big Al (bacon, cheese, aioli) and Cockadoodle Oink (chicken, bacon, brie) are classics. Yes, there's always a queue, but it moves fast and is part of the experience. Open late for après-ski or post-adventure hunger. Also try Fergbaker next door for pies and pastries.

reviews.summary: An institution — most visitors agree the burgers live up to the hype. The queue can be 30+ minutes at peak times. Some feel it's overhyped, but the quality-to-price ratio is excellent.

pois.name: Cargo Brewery
pois.short: Queenstown's waterfront craft brewery — Lake Wakatipu views, rotating taps and wood-fired pizzas.
pois.commentary: Cargo Brewery occupies a prime spot on Queenstown's Steamer Wharf with views across Lake Wakatipu to the Remarkables. The rotating taps feature their own beers alongside guest craft brews, and the wood-fired pizzas are a solid pairing. The outdoor terrace on a sunny afternoon is hard to beat — watch the TSS Earnslaw steam past while sipping a fresh pale ale. A relaxed counterpoint to Queenstown's adventure scene.

reviews.summary: The lakeside location is the standout. The beers are solid if not boundary-pushing. Good for a casual afternoon — don't expect a craft-beer destination.

pois.name: Botswana Butchery
pois.short: Queenstown's premium steak and wine restaurant — lakefront heritage building with an extensive NZ wine list.
pois.commentary: Botswana Butchery occupies a handsome stone heritage building on Queenstown's lakefront, serving premium NZ steaks, lamb and seafood. The wine list is one of the deepest in the South Island, with extensive Central Otago and NZ representation. The interior — leather banquettes, dark wood, moody lighting — creates an atmosphere that's both refined and welcoming. A go-to for a special dinner in Queenstown.

reviews.summary: The steak quality and wine list earn consistent praise. The heritage building is atmospheric. Prices are at the top end of Queenstown dining.

pois.name: Mt Rosa Wines
pois.short: Small-batch Gibbston winery — intimate tastings and estate-only Pinot Noir with mountain views.
pois.commentary: Mt Rosa is one of Gibbston's smaller, more personal wineries. The cellar door sits among the vines with mountain views, and tastings are intimate affairs — you'll often be served by the winemaker. The Pinot Noir is concentrated and elegant, reflecting the high-altitude Gibbston terroir. Much of the production sells at the cellar door, so visiting is often the only way to try the wines.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers appreciate the personal, unhurried experience. The Pinot Noir is impressive for the price. The small scale means limited hours — check ahead.

pois.name: Arrowtown Food Precinct
pois.short: Historic gold-mining village with a charming main street of restaurants, cafés and bars — 20 minutes from Queenstown.
pois.commentary: Arrowtown's Buckingham Street is one of New Zealand's most photogenic main streets, lined with heritage buildings housing excellent restaurants and cafés. Aosta (Italian fine dining), The Fork & Tap (craft beer and burgers) and Provisions (specialty coffee and pastries) are highlights. The village has a different energy from Queenstown — more refined, less frenetic. A lovely afternoon outing, especially in autumn when the trees turn gold.

reviews.summary: The heritage charm and food quality impress. A welcome escape from Queenstown's bustle. Can be very busy on autumn weekends for the colours.

pois.name: The Sherwood Kitchen
pois.short: Queenstown's sustainable dining pioneer — organic garden-to-plate food with mountain and lake views.
pois.commentary: The Sherwood is Queenstown's sustainability champion — an eco-hotel with a restaurant that grows much of its own produce in an organic garden. The menu changes daily based on what's ready to harvest, supplemented by local farmers and fishers. The setting — above Lake Wakatipu with mountain views — is spectacular, and the commitment to minimal waste is genuine. A refreshing alternative to Queenstown's commercial dining scene.

reviews.summary: The sustainability concept is executed beautifully. The garden-to-plate food is creative and delicious. Some find the location out of central Queenstown inconvenient.

pois.name: Gibbston River Trail
pois.short: Cycle trail along the Kawarau River linking Gibbston's wineries — ride between cellar doors on a scenic valley path.
pois.commentary: The Gibbston River Trail is one of the most scenic wine-by-bike experiences in the world. The trail follows the Kawarau River through the dramatic gorge, linking several Gibbston wineries — Chard Farm, Gibbston Valley, Kinross and more. E-bikes are available for hire and make the gentle terrain effortless. It's the smartest way to do wine tasting without worrying about driving, and the scenery is spectacular throughout.

reviews.summary: A brilliant way to combine cycling with wine tasting. The Kawarau Gorge scenery is breathtaking. Some sections can be busy with tour groups in summer.

pois.name: Mrs Woolly's General Store & Café
pois.short: Arrowtown's beloved general store café — specialty coffee, artisan provisions and heritage charm.
pois.commentary: Mrs Woolly's occupies a heritage building on Arrowtown's Buckingham Street, combining a specialty café with a curated general store of NZ artisan products. The coffee is among the best in the region, the cabinet food is excellent, and the shelves are stocked with local preserves, cheeses and gifts. It's the kind of place where you go for a coffee and leave with a bag of provisions.

reviews.summary: Arrowtown charm at its best. The coffee and cabinet food are excellent. The artisan products make great gifts. Can be crowded in peak season.

pois.name: Provisions of Arrowtown
pois.short: Arrowtown's specialty coffee and pastry shop — artisan baking, single-origin coffee and a morning ritual.
pois.commentary: Provisions has earned a devoted following in Arrowtown for its expertly prepared single-origin coffee and artisan pastries. The croissants and sourdough are baked fresh daily, and the seasonal tarts and cakes are a step above. It's small — just a counter and a few seats — so most people grab and go. Pair a flat white with a freshly baked croissant for one of the best morning starts in the region.

reviews.summary: The best coffee and pastries in Arrowtown. Small space means taking away is the norm. The croissants sell out early.

pois.name: Kiwi Birdlife Park
pois.short: See kiwi, tuatara, kea, and native bush birds in a compact wildlife park beside Lake Wakatipu.
pois.commentary: Kiwi Birdlife Park in central Queenstown offers close encounters with kiwi in a nocturnal house, plus tuatara, kea, morepork, and native bush birds. The conservation show is informative and entertaining. A great family activity in the heart of Queenstown.

reviews.summary: Reliable kiwi viewing in central Queenstown. Good for families. The kea are entertaining.

pois.name: Routeburn Track — Alpine Bird Country
pois.short: Great Walk through mountain beech forest and alpine meadows — kea, rock wren, and whio territory.
pois.commentary: The Routeburn Track passes through habitats ranging from beech forest to alpine herbfields. Kea are common around Harris Saddle, rock wren inhabit the alpine zone, and whio (blue duck) live in the valley streams. The bird diversity on multi-day walks is outstanding.

reviews.summary: World-class tramping with great birdlife. Kea encounters at Harris Saddle are memorable. Book huts well ahead.

pois.name: Dart River — Blue Duck (Whio) Habitat
pois.short: Pristine river valley where the endangered whio (blue duck) lives — jet boat and kayak access.
pois.commentary: The Dart River valley near Glenorchy is prime whio (blue duck) habitat. These rare ducks live only on clean, fast-flowing rivers, making the Dart one of their strongholds. Jet boat operators sometimes spot whio on the riverbanks. Kayaking offers quieter viewing opportunities.

reviews.summary: Beautiful river setting. Whio sightings are a bonus — they're well camouflaged. Jet boat trip is spectacular.

pois.name: Milford Sound — Bottlenose Dolphins & Seals
pois.short: Bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, and Fiordland crested penguins in the iconic fiord.
pois.commentary: Milford Sound cruises regularly encounter bottlenose dolphins, NZ fur seal colonies on the rocks, and occasionally rare Fiordland crested penguins. The marine environment of the fiord — with a freshwater layer over seawater — creates unique viewing conditions. Multiple cruise operators.

reviews.summary: The wildlife adds another dimension to the scenery. Seal sightings are almost guaranteed. Penguins are a lucky bonus.

pois.name: Glenorchy Lagoon Walkway — Wetland Birds
pois.short: Easy boardwalk around a scenic lagoon — paradise shelduck, scaup, and crested grebe with mountain backdrop.
pois.commentary: The Glenorchy Lagoon boardwalk circles a beautiful wetland at the head of Lake Wakatipu. Paradise shelduck, NZ scaup, grey teal, and Australasian crested grebe are regularly seen. The mountain backdrop — Earnslaw and the Humboldt Range — makes this one of NZ's most scenic bird walks.

reviews.summary: Beautiful setting for an easy walk. The crested grebes are a highlight. Mountain reflections on calm mornings.

pois.name: Bob's Cove — Wetland & Beech Forest Walk
pois.short: Lakeside wetland and beech forest walk near Queenstown — bellbird, fantail, and rifleman in the bush.
pois.commentary: Bob's Cove between Queenstown and Glenorchy offers a short loop walk through beech forest and lakeside wetland. The bush is alive with bellbird, fantail, rifleman, and tomtit. The wetland attracts grey teal, paradise shelduck, and pied stilt.

reviews.summary: Lovely short walk with good bird variety. Easy to access between Queenstown and Glenorchy. Peaceful.

pois.name: Moke Lake — Mountain Lake Birding
pois.short: Scenic mountain lake with paradise shelduck, NZ scaup, and bush birds in the surrounding forest.
pois.commentary: Moke Lake is a picturesque mountain lake 10 km from Queenstown. The lake margins attract paradise shelduck, NZ scaup, and grey teal. The surrounding bush holds bellbird, grey warbler, and fantail. The loop walk is easy and scenic.

reviews.summary: Peaceful alternative to busy Queenstown. Good bird variety. The loop walk is pleasant. Free camping available.

pois.name: Lake Wakatipu — Crested Grebe Viewing
pois.short: Australasian crested grebes breed on Lake Wakatipu — watch their elaborate courtship displays.
pois.commentary: Lake Wakatipu supports a population of Australasian crested grebes (kāmana), estimated at fewer than 500 nationally. The Queenstown Gardens lakefront and Frankton Arm are good viewing spots. Their elaborate courtship dance, with synchronized head-shaking and weed-carrying, is spectacular.

reviews.summary: Unusual bird viewing in an urban setting. The grebes are beautiful. Patience needed to spot them on the large lake.

pois.name: Rees Valley — Kea Country
pois.short: Mountain valley walk where kea, rock wren, and NZ falcon patrol the alpine zone.
pois.commentary: The Rees Valley track from Glenorchy enters prime kea habitat as it climbs towards the Rees Saddle. Kea are commonly seen around the mountain huts, NZ falcon (kārearea) hunt the tussock, and rock wren inhabit the high ground. The valley scenery is spectacular.

reviews.summary: Excellent alpine birding in stunning scenery. Kea are reliable around the huts. Multi-day tramp for full experience.

pois.name: Twelve Mile Delta — Lakeside Birding
pois.short: Lakeside delta area with diverse waterbirds — shoveler, crested grebe, and paradise shelduck.
pois.commentary: The Twelve Mile Delta on Lake Wakatipu is a popular camping and picnic area that also offers excellent waterbird viewing. NZ shoveler, Australasian crested grebe, paradise shelduck, and black shag are regular. The adjacent bush has bellbird and grey warbler.

reviews.summary: Easy lakeside birding. Good variety. Combine with a picnic. Free camping area nearby.

pois.name: Lakes District Museum Arrowtown
pois.short: Award-winning museum in historic Arrowtown telling the stories of gold rush, Chinese settlers, and pioneer life.
pois.commentary: The Lakes District Museum in the heart of Arrowtown's historic main street is one of New Zealand's most engaging regional museums. The museum tells the rich story of the Wakatipu Basin from early Māori occupation through the tumultuous gold rush of the 1860s to the development of the modern resort region. Particularly powerful are the displays on the Chinese gold miners who endured discrimination while making a vital contribution to the region. The museum complements the nearby Chinese Settlement — a restored encampment of stone huts where Chinese miners lived. Interactive gold panning experiences let visitors try their luck in the adjacent Arrow River. Arrowtown's main street, Buckingham Street, is one of the most charming heritage streetscapes in the South Island, lined with original gold-rush-era buildings now housing boutique shops and cafés. The museum has won multiple awards for its exhibitions and education programmes. Allow 1-1.5 hours for the museum, longer to explore Arrowtown.

reviews.summary: A fantastic museum that really brings the gold rush era to life. The Chinese miners story is deeply moving. Gold panning is great fun for kids. Arrowtown itself is charming — make a half-day of it.

pois.name: Arrowtown Creative Quarter
pois.short: Cluster of art galleries, studios, and boutiques in Arrowtown's charming heritage village streetscape.
pois.commentary: Arrowtown's creative quarter centres on Buckingham Street, one of the South Island's most picturesque heritage main streets. The gold-rush-era stone and timber buildings house a growing cluster of art galleries, artisan studios, craft shops, and design boutiques that have made the village a creative destination in its own right. Galleries feature works by local and regional artists, from landscape painting and photography to contemporary sculpture and jewellery. The village attracts serious artists drawn by the stunning mountain scenery and the quality of light. Studio visits and artist meet-and-greets are sometimes possible. The heritage architecture — including original miners' cottages, the former bank buildings, and the stone-built shops — creates a unique and atmospheric backdrop for browsing. Arrowtown is just 20 minutes from Queenstown but feels like a different world. The autumn colours (April-May) when the deciduous trees blaze gold and red are spectacular. Combine gallery browsing with a walk along the Arrow River trail.

reviews.summary: Arrowtown is absolutely charming and the galleries are a real highlight. The quality of art is surprisingly high for a small village. Visit in autumn for the incredible golden trees and perfect walking weather.

pois.name: Skyline Stargazing Queenstown
pois.short: Guided stargazing experience at the Skyline Gondola summit with powerful telescopes and Māori star lore.
pois.commentary: Skyline Stargazing offers a unique night-sky experience from the summit of Bob's Peak above Queenstown, accessed by the Skyline Gondola. The guided stargazing sessions use high-powered telescopes and expert guides to reveal the wonders of the southern hemisphere sky, including the Southern Cross, Magellanic Clouds, star clusters, and planets. Sessions incorporate Māori star lore (tātai arorangi), adding a unique cultural dimension to the astronomical experience. The elevated location above Queenstown's lights, combined with the region's clear mountain air, provides excellent stargazing conditions. Sessions run on clear evenings year-round, with the best conditions in autumn and winter when nights are longer and darker. The experience includes the gondola ride, which itself offers stunning views of Queenstown's lights reflected in Lake Wakatipu. Sessions last approximately 1-1.5 hours. Bookings are essential and are weather-dependent — have a backup plan for cloudy nights. Dress warmly as summit temperatures can be significantly colder than town.

reviews.summary: A magical experience seeing the southern stars through powerful telescopes. The Māori astronomy stories add wonderful cultural depth. The gondola ride up with Queenstown lit below is special in itself. Dress very warmly!

pois.name: Jack's Point Track
pois.short: Scenic lakeside track south of Queenstown with dramatic Remarkables mountain views and peaceful bush walking.
pois.commentary: Jack's Point Track is a rewarding walking track on the southern shore of Lake Wakatipu, offering spectacular views of The Remarkables mountain range without the crowds of more famous Queenstown walks. The track traverses a mix of open tussock, rocky outcrops, and native bush along the lake shore, with the sheer wall of The Remarkables rising dramatically across the water. The full loop track takes approximately 2-3 hours and includes some moderate climbing. Several viewpoints along the way offer perfect spots for photography and rest. The track is well maintained and suitable for reasonably fit walkers. The area is rich in birdlife, and you may spot fantails, bellbirds, and tūī in the bush sections. The trailhead is located within the Jack's Point residential development, about 15 minutes south of Queenstown. Start at the Jack's Point clubhouse car park. The track is accessible year-round, though winter conditions may be icy in places. An excellent alternative to the busier Queenstown Hill and Ben Lomond tracks.

reviews.summary: Stunning views of The Remarkables from a lovely, uncrowded track. A refreshing alternative to the busier Queenstown walks. The lakeside sections are particularly beautiful.

pois.name: Canyon Fox Zipline Queenstown
pois.short: Thrilling zipline experience flying across a deep canyon with Queenstown and lake views below.
pois.commentary: Canyon Fox is one of Queenstown's most accessible adrenaline activities, offering a series of ziplines that send riders flying across a deep canyon on the slopes above town. The experience combines multiple zipline stages with canyon crossings, reaching speeds of up to 70km/h while suspended high above the canyon floor. Riders enjoy sweeping views of Queenstown, Lake Wakatipu, and the surrounding mountains as they zip between platforms. The activity is suitable for a wide range of ages and fitness levels, making it a good option for families or groups where not everyone wants to do a bungy jump or skydive. The experience takes about 1.5 hours including safety briefing and gear fitting. Ziplining requires no special skills — just the courage to step off the platform. The operation runs year-round from the Skyline Gondola complex on Bob's Peak. Weight and age restrictions apply. Book online for best pricing and check for combo deals with the gondola and luge.

reviews.summary: A real thrill without being as terrifying as bungy! The canyon views are spectacular and the speed is exhilarating. Great for families wanting an adventure activity everyone can enjoy.

pois.name: Gibbston Valley Winery
pois.short: Pioneering Central Otago winery with NZ's largest wine cave, restaurant, and cheesery in the Gibbston Valley.
pois.commentary: Gibbston Valley was the pioneer of Central Otago winemaking, planting its first vines in 1981 when the region was considered too cold and remote for viticulture. Today it anchors the Gibbston Valley wine cluster, known as the Valley of the Vines, just 25 minutes from Queenstown. The winery's most distinctive feature is New Zealand's largest wine cave, carved 76 metres into the schist rock hillside, where wines age at a constant cool temperature. Cave tours and tastings are a highlight. The cellar door offers tastings of their acclaimed Pinot Noir alongside other varieties. The on-site restaurant serves seasonal cuisine, and the adjacent cheesery produces artisan cheeses. A cycle trail connects several Gibbston wineries, and bike hire is available. The dramatic valley setting, framed by rugged mountains and the Kawarau Gorge, makes this a scenic and rewarding wine experience.

reviews.summary: The wine cave experience is unique and fascinating. The Pinot Noir is consistently excellent and the cheesery is a bonus. Perfect as part of a Gibbston Valley wine cycling day.

pois.name: Amisfield Winery & Bistro
pois.short: Premium Queenstown winery and bistro on Lake Hayes with award-winning Pinot Noir and Trust the Chef menu.
pois.commentary: Amisfield is one of the Queenstown region's most celebrated wine and dining destinations, set on a striking property overlooking Lake Hayes with The Remarkables mountain range as a backdrop. The winery produces premium Central Otago wines with the Pinot Noir as its flagship, consistently earning critical acclaim. The bistro offers a unique 'Trust the Chef' dining experience where the kitchen creates a multi-course meal based on the best seasonal ingredients available, paired with Amisfield wines. This approach delivers creative, surprising dishes that showcase Central Otago produce. The tasting room offers flights and individual glass tastings in a sophisticated yet relaxed setting. The architecture blends stone and glass, complementing the dramatic landscape. Reservations for the bistro are essential, especially during ski season and summer. Located 15 minutes from Queenstown on the road to Arrowtown.

reviews.summary: A world-class dining and wine experience in a stunning setting. The Trust the Chef menu is a culinary adventure. The Pinot Noir is superb and the lake and mountain views are magnificent.

pois.name: Peregrine Wines
pois.short: Architecturally striking Gibbston Valley winery with a wing-shaped roof and acclaimed Pinot Noir wines.
pois.commentary: Peregrine Wines is immediately recognisable by its award-winning winery building, featuring a dramatic wing-shaped roof inspired by the peregrine falcon in flight. Located in the heart of the Gibbston Valley, the architectural landmark houses a modern cellar door where visitors can taste across the Peregrine range. The Pinot Noir is the star, benefiting from the valley's unique microclimate of warm days and cool nights at altitude. The Pinot Gris and Riesling are also noteworthy. Tastings are conducted by knowledgeable staff who explain the influence of the schist soils and continental climate on the wines. The outdoor terrace offers views across the valley to the surrounding mountains. Peregrine is part of the Gibbston Valley wine cluster, easily visited alongside neighbouring wineries by car or the popular cycle trail. A refined, elegant wine experience in a spectacular setting.

reviews.summary: The striking winery building alone is worth the visit, and the wines match the architectural ambition. The Pinot Noir is elegant and complex. A highlight of the Gibbston Valley wine trail.

pois.name: Nevis Swing
pois.short: The world's biggest canyon swing, a 160-metre arc across the Nevis River canyon near Queenstown.
pois.commentary: The Nevis Swing holds the title of the world's biggest canyon swing, sending riders on a massive 160-metre arc across the rugged Nevis River gorge. Operated by AJ Hackett, the swing is located at the same remote canyon site as the Nevis Bungy. After a scenic 4WD journey into the backcountry, participants are strapped into a harness and released from a platform 160 metres above the river. Unlike bungy where you fall vertically, the swing creates a sweeping pendulum motion across the canyon, reaching speeds of up to 150 km/h. Solo, tandem, and various body positions are available, each offering a different thrill. The transfer from Queenstown takes about 45 minutes and passes through stunning high-country scenery. Minimum weight is 35kg and maximum is 235kg for solo riders. All equipment and safety briefings are provided.

reviews.summary: An absolutely terrifying and exhilarating experience. The scale of the canyon and the arc of the swing are beyond anything else available. The 4WD journey to the site adds to the adventure.

pois.name: Dart River Safari
pois.short: Jet boat safari into the pristine Dart River wilderness, a glacier-fed river in Mount Aspiring National Park.
pois.commentary: The Dart River Safari combines a thrilling jet boat ride with a guided wilderness experience deep into the heart of Mount Aspiring National Park. The journey begins in Glenorchy, 45 minutes from Queenstown, and ventures up the glacier-fed Dart River through ancient beech forest, past towering peaks, and into landscapes used as filming locations for The Lord of the Rings. The jet boat navigates braided river channels just centimetres deep, spinning and weaving through the river's ever-changing course. A guided bush walk reveals the ecology of this pristine environment, including native birds and ancient trees. The return journey includes a scenic drive or optional funyak inflatable canoe paddle down the river. The combination of adrenaline, natural beauty, and wilderness immersion makes this one of Queenstown's most complete adventure experiences. Weather dependent, with departures year-round.

reviews.summary: An incredible wilderness experience that combines adventure with stunning natural beauty. The Dart River valley is breathtaking and the jet boating is thrilling. Guides are passionate and knowledgeable about the area.

pois.name: Mrs Woolly's General Store & Cheese
pois.short: Artisan cheese shop and general store in historic Arrowtown with local and NZ specialty cheeses.
pois.commentary: Mrs Woolly's General Store is a charming artisan food shop on Arrowtown's historic Buckingham Street, specialising in New Zealand artisan cheeses alongside a curated selection of local produce, smallgoods, preserves, and pantry items. The cheese counter features selections from Central Otago and other NZ producers, with staff happy to guide tastings and recommend pairings. The store captures the character of Arrowtown's gold-rush-era streetscape while offering contemporary gourmet products. Cheese boards and platters can be prepared for picnics in the nearby riverside park or to take on a Gibbston Valley wine tour. The shop also stocks local honey, olive oils, and artisan chocolates, making it an excellent source of edible souvenirs. Arrowtown's tree-lined historic precinct offers boutique shopping and excellent cafes within a few minutes' walk. A delightful stop in one of the region's prettiest towns.

reviews.summary: A wonderful artisan food shop in a beautiful setting. The cheese selection is impressive and the picnic platters are perfect for a Gibbston wine day. Charming and well-curated.

pois.name: Skyline Gondola & Luge
pois.short: Gondola ride to Bob's Peak with panoramic views and downhill luge tracks overlooking the lake and mountains.
pois.commentary: The Skyline Gondola whisks visitors 450 metres above Queenstown to the top of Bob's Peak, providing one of the most spectacular viewpoints in New Zealand. From the viewing deck, a sweeping panorama takes in Lake Wakatipu, the Remarkables, Cecil Peak, Walter Peak, and Queenstown township far below. The Skyline Luge offers three downhill cart tracks of varying difficulty that wind down the mountain through native bush and open terrain, with riders controlling speed via handlebars. Multiple luge rides are included in combo passes, and the chairlift returns riders to the top for another run. The summit complex includes a restaurant, cafe, and gift shop, with the Stratosfare Restaurant offering fine dining with the view. Stargazing sessions operate on clear evenings. Mountain biking trails accessible from the gondola provide another downhill option. The gondola operates year-round from the base station on Brecon Street in central Queenstown.

reviews.summary: Spectacular views from the gondola and the luge is addictively fun for all ages. The multi-ride luge passes offer great value. The restaurant view is one of New Zealand's finest dining panoramas.

pois.name: Lakes District Museum & Gallery, Arrowtown
pois.short: Award-winning Arrowtown museum telling the story of the gold rush, Maori settlement, and European pioneers — with gold panning in the Arrow River outside.
pois.commentary: The Lakes District Museum sits at the heart of Arrowtown's Buckingham Street, occupying the old Bank of New Zealand building. Its permanent collection traces the Otago gold rush from the first strikes in 1862 through the Chinese miner era and into the pastoral age that followed. Interactive exhibits let you try your hand at gold panning in the Arrow River, and the gallery space hosts rotating exhibitions of regional art. The museum has won multiple New Zealand museum awards for its interpretation and community engagement. It is an essential first stop before exploring Arrowtown's historic precinct.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently praise the Lakes District Museum for its engaging gold rush exhibits and hands-on gold panning. The gallery adds an unexpected cultural dimension to Arrowtown. Staff are knowledgeable and the entry fee is considered excellent value.

pois.name: Arrowtown Chinese Settlement
pois.short: Preserved remains of a Chinese miners' settlement from the 1860s gold rush — one of the most poignant heritage sites in the South Island.
pois.commentary: Tucked along Bush Creek at the edge of Arrowtown, the Chinese Settlement preserves the tiny stone and earth dwellings where Chinese miners lived during the Otago gold rush. Brought to New Zealand to rework claims abandoned by European miners, the Chinese community faced significant discrimination yet built a resilient village that endured for decades. DOC restored the site in the 1980s, rebuilding Ah Lum's store and several huts with careful archaeological research. Interpretive panels tell individual stories of the men who lived and died here far from home. The settlement is free to visit and deeply moving — a quiet counterpoint to Arrowtown's boutique charm.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe the Chinese Settlement as genuinely moving and historically important. The DOC restoration is praised for its sensitivity and the interpretive panels bring individual stories to life. A must-visit alongside the main Arrowtown strip.

pois.name: TSS Earnslaw Vintage Steamship
pois.short: A 1912 coal-fired steamship still plying Lake Wakatipu — the last commercial passenger coal-fired vessel in the Southern Hemisphere.
pois.commentary: The TSS Earnslaw was launched in 1912 to serve the remote sheep stations around Lake Wakatipu, carrying wool, supplies, and passengers where no roads existed. Over a century later, the twin-screw steamer still fires her original coal boilers for the crossing to Walter Peak High Country Farm. Below decks you can watch the stokers feed coal into the furnaces — a mesmerising, hot, and loud experience. The cruise across the lake with The Remarkables as a backdrop is one of Queenstown's most iconic experiences. At Walter Peak, a farmyard tour and afternoon tea or a gourmet barbecue dinner round out the trip.

reviews.summary: The TSS Earnslaw consistently earns high marks for its unique heritage experience on Lake Wakatipu. Watching the coal-fired engines work below deck is a highlight for all ages. The Walter Peak farm visit and barbecue dinner are frequently recommended.

pois.name: Routeburn Track
pois.short: One of New Zealand's ten Great Walks — a 32 km alpine crossing linking Mount Aspiring and Fiordland national parks.
pois.commentary: The Routeburn Track climbs from beech forest in the Dart Valley to the Harris Saddle at 1,255 metres, then descends through the Hollyford Valley to the Divide on the Milford Road. The alpine section above the bushline delivers some of the most spectacular scenery in the country — the Darran Mountains, Lake Harris, and on a clear day views to Martins Bay on the coast. Three DOC huts break the journey into comfortable day-stages. The track is typically walked November to April, with hut bookings essential in the Great Walks season. Weather can change rapidly at altitude — alpine gear is required even in summer.

reviews.summary: Trampers rate the Routeburn as one of New Zealand's finest multi-day walks, with the alpine section above Harris Saddle a particular highlight. Hut facilities are well maintained. Bookings fill quickly — planning months ahead is essential.

pois.name: Central Art Gallery Queenstown
pois.short: Queenstown's oldest privately owned gallery — showcasing New Zealand contemporary and traditional art on the lakefront.
pois.commentary: Central Art Gallery has been a quiet anchor of Queenstown's cultural scene for decades, occupying a lakefront space that most visitors walk past on the way to the wharf. Inside, the collection spans New Zealand landscapes, abstract works, and sculpture by established and emerging artists. The gallery represents painters who capture the Otago light and mountain scenery that defines this region. Staff are approachable and happy to discuss the works without pressure. It is a welcome respite from Queenstown's adrenaline-fuelled main street.

reviews.summary: Art lovers appreciate the quality curation and lakefront setting. The gallery offers a calm counterpoint to Queenstown's adventure scene. Some wish for a larger exhibition space but praise the rotating shows.

pois.name: Ben Lomond Summit Track
pois.short: Queenstown's signature day hike — a challenging climb to the 1,748 m summit with 360-degree views of Lake Wakatipu and the surrounding ranges.
pois.commentary: Ben Lomond rises directly behind Queenstown, and the summit track is the big day walk for fit trampers. Most hikers take the gondola to Bob's Peak to shorten the ascent, then climb through sub-alpine scrub to the saddle before the final steep push to the 1,748 m summit. The reward is a panorama that takes in Lake Wakatipu end to end, The Remarkables, Cecil Peak, Walter Peak, and on a clear day the Darran Mountains of Fiordland. Allow six to eight hours return from the gondola top station. The track is exposed above the bushline — warm layers, waterproofs, and sun protection are essential even in summer.

reviews.summary: Hikers rave about the summit views, calling them among the best day-walk panoramas in the South Island. The final section is steep and exposed. Taking the gondola up saves significant time and energy.

pois.name: Gibbston Valley Wine Region
pois.short: The heart of Central Otago's Pinot Noir country — a sheltered river valley 25 minutes from Queenstown lined with cellar doors.
pois.commentary: Gibbston Valley sits in a narrow schist gorge along the Kawarau River, where the microclimate of hot summers and cold winters produces some of New Zealand's most celebrated Pinot Noir. The original Gibbston Valley Winery pioneered the area in 1981 and still operates a wine cave carved into the hillside for barrel aging. Today a string of cellar doors — Peregrine, Chard Farm, Kinross, Mt Rosa — line the valley and the cycling trail that connects them. The Gibbston River Trail makes a leisurely bike ride between tastings. For wine lovers, this is the essential Queenstown side trip.

reviews.summary: Wine enthusiasts praise the concentrated cluster of excellent cellar doors and the dramatic gorge setting. The bike trail between wineries is a highlight. Pinot Noir from this valley consistently ranks among New Zealand's best.

pois.name: Glenorchy Lagoon Walkway
pois.short: A boardwalk through pristine wetland at the head of Lake Wakatipu — mountains reflected in still water and abundant birdlife.
pois.commentary: The Glenorchy Lagoon Walkway loops through a wetland ecosystem at the northern tip of Lake Wakatipu, where the Rees and Dart rivers feed into the lake. The boardwalk keeps your feet dry while giving intimate access to a landscape of marsh, tussock, and mirror-still water backed by snow-capped mountains. Birdwatchers come for the paradise shelducks, grey teal, and occasional Australasian bittern. Early morning and late afternoon light transforms the lagoon into a photographer's paradise — the mountain reflections on calm days are extraordinary. The walk is flat, accessible, and takes about an hour at a relaxed pace.

reviews.summary: Photographers and nature lovers rate this as one of the most beautiful short walks in the Queenstown region. The mountain reflections on still mornings are world-class. Easy and accessible for all fitness levels.

pois.name: Skippers Canyon
pois.short: A notorious gold rush canyon with a single-lane road carved into sheer cliff faces — not for the faint-hearted, and not covered by rental car insurance.
pois.commentary: Skippers Canyon was the scene of frenzied gold mining in the 1860s, and the road built to service it remains one of the most hair-raising drives in New Zealand. The single-lane gravel road clings to near-vertical bluffs above the Shotover River, with sheer drops and no barriers. Most rental car companies explicitly exclude this road from their insurance. Guided 4WD tours are the safest way to experience it, combining the white-knuckle drive with gold rush history and views of the Long Gully suspension bridge. The canyon itself is beautiful — deep, narrow, and lined with schist cliffs that glow gold in afternoon light.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the canyon road as both terrifying and exhilarating. The gold rush history adds depth to the experience. Guided 4WD tours are strongly recommended over self-driving.

pois.name: Vesta Design Museum & Cafe
pois.short: A design museum, gallery, and cafe in a beautifully adapted heritage building on Arrowtown's main street.
pois.commentary: Vesta occupies one of Arrowtown's heritage buildings on Buckingham Street, transformed into a space where design, art, and food intersect. The gallery showcases New Zealand and international design — furniture, ceramics, jewellery, and textiles — in curated exhibitions that change regularly. The cafe serves excellent coffee and seasonal fare in a space that feels like an extension of the gallery. The adaptive reuse of the historic building itself is part of the experience — original features sit alongside contemporary interventions. Vesta brings a design-conscious sensibility to a town better known for gold rush history.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the combination of design gallery and quality cafe in a heritage setting. The exhibitions are thoughtfully curated and regularly refreshed. The coffee and food are praised as among Arrowtown's best.

pois.name: Moke Lake
pois.short: A peaceful mountain lake just 15 minutes from Queenstown — popular for swimming, camping, and escaping the crowds.
pois.commentary: Moke Lake sits in a high valley surrounded by tussock-covered hills, remarkably close to Queenstown yet worlds away from the bustle. The DOC campsite on the shore is one of the most scenic budget camping spots in the region. In summer the lake is warm enough for swimming, and the loop track around the shore takes about an hour. The access road is gravel but manageable for most vehicles. Moke Lake is the locals' escape — a place to picnic, fish, or simply sit by the water with mountain views in every direction. Early morning visits offer the best reflections and solitude.

reviews.summary: Locals and visitors alike praise Moke Lake as a hidden gem so close to Queenstown. The camping is basic but the setting is spectacular. Swimming on a warm day with mountain views is a highlight.

pois.name: Dart River Wilderness Jet
pois.short: A jet boat journey into pristine wilderness up the glacier-fed Dart River — through landscapes used as Middle-earth filming locations.
pois.commentary: The Dart River Wilderness Jet takes you from Glenorchy deep into the heart of Mount Aspiring National Park, navigating braided glacier-fed channels that only a jet boat can reach. The river runs through ancient beech forest and past towering mountain walls — several locations here doubled as Isengard and other Middle-earth settings in the Lord of the Rings films. The experience combines adrenaline-pumping jet boat manoeuvres with moments of stillness in genuinely remote wilderness. A bush walk and funyak (inflatable canoe) paddle back through side channels complete the half-day adventure. This is Queenstown's most immersive wilderness experience.

reviews.summary: Reviewers call this the best jet boat experience in the Queenstown area for its combination of wilderness scenery and adventure. LOTR fans love the filming location connections. The funyak section adds a peaceful contrast to the jet boat thrills.

pois.name: Jack's Point Track
pois.short: A lakefront trail south of Queenstown with unobstructed views of The Remarkables — one of the region's best easy walks.
pois.commentary: Jack's Point Track runs along the eastern shore of Lake Wakatipu south of Frankton, offering what many consider the finest accessible views of The Remarkables mountain range. The trail is mostly flat, winding through tussock grassland along the lake edge with the dramatic sawtooth ridgeline directly ahead. It is accessible to walkers of all fitness levels and particularly rewarding in late afternoon when the mountains catch golden light. The track connects to the larger network around Jack's Point residential area. Bring a camera — the combination of lake, tussock, and mountains produces some of the most photogenic compositions in the Queenstown region.

reviews.summary: Walkers praise the easy access and stunning Remarkables views. The flat terrain makes it suitable for families and all fitness levels. Late afternoon light is particularly recommended for photography.

pois.name: Onsen Hot Pools
pois.short: Private cedar-lined hot tubs perched above the Shotover Canyon — luxury soaking with mountain views by day or stars by night.
pois.commentary: Onsen Hot Pools offers private hot-pool experiences in cedar-lined tubs cantilevered over the Shotover River canyon between Queenstown and Arrowtown. Each pool is enclosed for privacy but open to the sky and the mountain views, making it equally magical at sunset or under stars. The water is kept at a perfect 38-39 degrees Celsius, and the minimalist Japanese-inspired design creates an atmosphere of calm luxury. Booking is essential — sessions are timed and the pools are popular year-round. It is one of Queenstown's few genuinely relaxing experiences in a town built on adrenaline. Night sessions under clear skies are particularly recommended.

reviews.summary: Guests consistently describe Onsen as the perfect Queenstown relaxation experience. The canyon setting and mountain views elevate it beyond a typical spa. Night sessions with stars overhead are rated as unforgettable.

pois.name: Coronet Peak Summer Activities
pois.short: Queenstown's closest ski field transforms in summer — mountain biking, tandem paragliding, and scenic chairlift rides with panoramic views.
pois.commentary: Coronet Peak sits just 20 minutes from Queenstown and reinvents itself each summer as an adventure hub. The scenic chairlift carries visitors to 1,200 metres for sweeping views across the Wakatipu Basin, The Remarkables, and the Crown Range. Mountain bikers access a network of purpose-built trails from the summit, ranging from flowy intermediate runs to technical descents. Tandem paragliding launches from the upper slopes offer a bird's-eye perspective on the entire region. The base building cafe and picnic areas make it a comfortable half-day outing. Summer operations typically run from December through March, weather dependent.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the summer transformation of the ski field, with the chairlift views and mountain biking most praised. Tandem paragliding gets rave reviews for the panoramic experience. Check operating days before visiting as schedules vary.

pois.name: Mou Waho Island Nature Reserve
pois.short: A predator-free island sanctuary in the middle of Lake Wanaka — home to rare buff weka and a ‘lake within a lake’.
pois.commentary: Mou Waho is a DOC-managed nature reserve in Lake Wanaka. The island supports populations of buff weka found nowhere else. A guided walk climbs to the summit where an alpine tarn creates a famous ‘lake within a lake’ photograph.

reviews.summary: The ‘lake within a lake’ is remarkable. Buff weka are tame and photogenic.

pois.name: Roy’s Peak Track
pois.short: The most Instagrammed hike in NZ — a steep climb to a 1,578 m summit with panoramic views over Lake Wanaka.
pois.commentary: Roy’s Peak is 16 km return with 1,300 m elevation gain. The famous ‘Instagram spot’ is actually a false summit about 30 minutes before the true peak. Closed during lambing season.

reviews.summary: The view is absolutely worth the effort. Start early to avoid crowds.

pois.name: Dart River — Te Awa Whakatipu Wilderness
pois.short: The braided Dart River flowing from the Southern Alps into the head of Lake Whakatipu — the most pristine glacial valley on the North Fiordland border.
pois.commentary: The Dart River — Te Awa Whakatipu — runs 60 km from the Whitbourn Glacier in the Main Divide to the head of Lake Whakatipu at Kinloch, forming the boundary between Mount Aspiring National Park and the Humboldt Mountains. The valley floor is a classic New Zealand braided river — multiple channels shifting across greywacke gravel through podocarp and beech forest. Dart River Journeys runs a combination jet-boat and walk that reaches the true wilderness valley beyond what any road serves. The *Lord of the Rings* Ithilien camp and parts of Lothlórien were filmed in the side valley of Paradise — 10 km down an unsealed road from Glenorchy, and a destination in its own right.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Dart valley as the most awe-inspiring landscape in the Queenstown region — the combination of braided river, beech forest, and uninterrupted alpine wall is called incomparable. The jet-boat trip is praised for access rather than just adrenaline. Reviewers consistently say it exceeds expectations.

pois.name: Moke Lake Loop Track
pois.short: A remote-feeling mountain lake 20 minutes from Queenstown — a 2-hour loop through tussock and beech above Lake Whakatipu.
pois.commentary: Moke Lake is a small tarn-like lake in a valley immediately west of Queenstown, reached by an unsealed road off the Glenorchy highway. The 2-hour loop track climbs through tussock grassland and silver beech scrub with views back to the Remarkables and across Lake Whakatipu. The lake itself is used for swimming (cold but clear) and trout fishing. It feels entirely remote despite being 25 km from the Queenstown CBD. A small DOC campsite sits at the lake edge. This is the Queenstown walk for those who want to leave the crowds completely behind without going further than a day-trip allows. Very little signage on the Glenorchy highway — look for the Moke Lake turnoff about 10 km from Queenstown.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Moke Lake as Queenstown's best-kept secret — a genuinely wild-feeling lake and loop walk that takes under two hours total and is accessible in a normal car. Reviewers consistently note the absence of crowds is the headline feature. The campsite is called one of the best free DOC sites in the region.

pois.name: Closeburn Station — Whakatipu Conservation Area
pois.short: Beech forest and meadow walking on the western shore of Lake Whakatipu — a DOC conservation area accessed from the Glenorchy road.
pois.commentary: The western shore of Lake Whakatipu between Queenstown and Glenorchy is largely private station country with a series of DOC-managed walking areas interspersed. The Closeburn Conservation Area — managed as part of the Whakatipu Wildlife Habitat — provides access to undisturbed silver beech forest growing down to the lakeshore on the western terrace. The area supports kererū, kārearea (NZ falcon), and has historically been part of a managed kiwi translocation zone. A self-guided half-day walk combines lake-edge flats with beech hill forest. Accessed from a layby on the Glenorchy-Queenstown road, about 20 km north of Queenstown.

reviews.summary: Visitors who find the Closeburn walking area describe it as one of the Glenorchy road's underused assets — quiet beech forest walking with lake views that feel entirely different from the Queenstown tourist circuit. Reviewers note this is a walk where birdsong replaces the usual adventure-tourism soundtrack.

pois.name: Twelve Mile Delta Campsite & Track
pois.short: A DOC campsite on the shore of Lake Whakatipu 12 miles from Queenstown — beech forest, mountain biking, and the best free camp in the region.
pois.commentary: Twelve Mile Delta is a DOC campsite and recreation area on the western shore of Lake Whakatipu, 12 miles (19 km) north of Queenstown on the Glenorchy road. It sits in a river delta where Twelve Mile Creek fans out into the lake through a stand of silver beech — one of the most scenic campsite locations in the Queenstown Lakes district. The site has a network of mountain-bike and walking tracks through the beech forest that connect south to the Queenstown Trail cycle network. Facilities are basic (toilets, water). The proximity to Queenstown (30 minutes) makes it paradoxically little-used. Kererū (wood pigeons) are commonly seen in the beech canopy.

reviews.summary: Campers describe Twelve Mile Delta as a revelation — one of the most scenic DOC campsites in the South Island, barely 30 minutes from Queenstown's busy centre. Reviewers note the beech forest walking and mountain biking tracks are a surprise addition to what appears to be just a campsite. Best midweek when the site is nearly empty.

pois.name: Cecil Peak Station Walk
pois.short: A high-country station walk up 1,978 m Cecil Peak — boat-access from Queenstown to one of the most demanding day-walks in the region.
pois.commentary: Cecil Peak rises to 1,978 m on the western shore of Lake Whakatipu directly opposite Queenstown. The peak is visible from the town and gondola but rarely climbed — it is private station land, and access requires booking a boat crossing from Queenstown Wharf. The Cecil Peak Sky Walk is the commercial version (guided, helicopter return option), but the independent approach via the landowner's permit path makes a demanding full-day tramp with continuous views. From the summit, the panorama spans from the head of the lake to Kingston — one of the great views in the district that most Queenstown visitors see only from the other side. Fit walkers only; arrange boat access in advance.

reviews.summary: Visitors who complete the Cecil Peak walk describe the summit views as the finest in the Queenstown basin — notably because you are looking across at the town and the Remarkables, rather than looking from them. The boat-access approach is praised for adding a sense of expedition. Reviewers note the crowds are non-existent by Queenstown standards.

pois.name: Mavora Lakes, Mararoa Valley
pois.short: Two remote, clear-water lakes in the Mararoa Valley — used as Nen Hithoel in The Lord of the Rings, and one of the quietest DOC campsites in the region.
pois.commentary: The Mavora Lakes — a pair of linked glacial lakes in the Mararoa Valley, 90 km north of Invercargill and 75 km west of Queenstown via rough gravel — were used as the filming location for Nen Hithoel (Anduin River above Rauros Falls) in *The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring*. They are extraordinarily transparent, fluorescent blue, and set in unbroken silver beech forest with the Thomson Mountains behind. The surrounding Mavora Lakes Conservation Park (39,000 ha) encompasses brown trout streams, wetlands, and sub-alpine tussock. A two-day tramp connects the lakes to Te Anau via the Mararoa River. One of the genuinely remote and uncommercialised great landscapes within reach of Queenstown.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Mavora Lakes as among the most beautiful and least-crowded places in the entire South Island — comparable scenically to better-known lakes but with a fraction of the visitors. The beech forest reflection in the water is called incomparable. Reviewers consistently note the gravel-road access keeps tour coaches away.

pois.name: Queenstown Trail — Frankton to Lake Hayes Loop
pois.short: A 26 km cycle loop through Frankton, around Lake Hayes, and back — one of New Zealand's 22 Great Rides, mostly flat, in e-bike country.
pois.commentary: The Queenstown Trail is one of New Zealand's Ngā Haerenga Great Rides — a network of sealed and gravel paths connecting Queenstown township, Frankton, Arrowtown, and Lake Hayes. The most popular section is the Frankton-Lake Hayes loop: about 26 km, mostly flat, passing through Frankton village and along the tranquil shores of Lake Hayes. The lake is a 2 km-long former river basin, often perfectly still in the morning, reflecting the Coronet Peak range. The trail is accessible on standard bikes but has become heavily e-bike country — hire available in Queenstown and Frankton. Best ridden from Queenstown in a half-day, combining with a late lunch at the Lake Hayes café cluster.

reviews.summary: Reviewers consistently call the Queenstown Trail's Lake Hayes loop one of the best day-cycling experiences in the South Island — the combination of good surface, manageable distance, and the lake scenery makes it accessible to a wide range of fitness levels. E-bike hire is praised for flattening what little elevation there is. Families rate it particularly highly.

pois.name: Queenstown Arts Centre & William Wiggins Esq
pois.short: Queenstown's central arts hub — a surprisingly serious contemporary gallery in the middle of the adventure-tourism downtown.
pois.commentary: The Queenstown Arts Centre on Rees Street operates as the district's public gallery space, with rotating exhibitions of New Zealand and international contemporary art. The associated William Wiggins Esq gallery on the same block shows work by represented artists with an emphasis on landscape painting responding to the Whakatipu basin — which attracts a serious cohort of New Zealand painters. Given Queenstown's reputation as a pure adventure-tourism hub, the quality of the arts offering regularly surprises visitors. The downtown Art Trail — a self-guided brochure available from the i-SITE — connects nine public sculptures and installations across the CBD, many of them by Ngāi Tahu artists.

reviews.summary: Reviewers are consistently surprised by the quality of Queenstown's contemporary art scene — visitors who spend time at the Arts Centre describe it as the most unexpected thing they did in town. The landscape painting tradition is called authentic and specific to the place. Reviewers note the Art Trail brochure is worth picking up from the i-SITE.

pois.name: Lakes District Museum & Art Gallery, Arrowtown
pois.short: The region's best small museum — the full gold-rush story, Chinese settlement interpretation, and an art gallery, all in one Arrowtown building.
pois.commentary: The Lakes District Museum on Buckingham Street, Arrowtown, is the definitive repository of Wakatipu history — gold-rush artefacts, Chinese miner personal items, pastoral-era objects, and an oral-history archive. The permanent exhibitions are compact but high-quality; the Chinese goldfields section is one of the finest interpretations of this history in New Zealand, built in consultation with the Chinese community. The attached art gallery holds rotating exhibitions of works inspired by the Central Otago landscape. Entry is a modest charge. The museum is the essential companion to the Arrowtown Chinese Settlement site 300 m away — visit the museum first, then walk the settlement.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently describe the Lakes District Museum as the highlight of their Arrowtown stop — compact, intelligent, and genuinely moving in its treatment of the Chinese goldfield story. Reviewers recommend allowing 90 minutes and visiting before the outdoor settlement. The art gallery is frequently cited as a bonus.

pois.name: Kā Tiritiri o Te Moana — Remarkables Kāi Tahu Cultural Landscape
pois.short: The Remarkables as Kāi Tahu know them — Kā Tiritiri o Te Moana, the 'peaks of the glistening sea', a landscape of spiritual and resource significance.
pois.commentary: The Remarkables — Kā Tiritiri o Te Moana in Kāi Tahu reo — are not merely a ski field backdrop. The name translates roughly as 'the jagged peaks reflected in the water', a descriptor that makes sense from the lake. For Kāi Tahu, the entire Whakatipu basin is a place of dense cultural meaning: Lake Whakatipu is the hollowed-out impression of a giant (Whakatipu-waka-māui means 'the heart of the demon whose heart still beats' — the lake rises and falls approximately 20 cm twice a day, explained as the heartbeat of the taniwha buried beneath). The Remarkables marked the skyline of seasonal hunting, gathering, and travel routes between coastal settlements and the Pounamu Coast. Ngāi Tahu's Kaikoura-based pounamu resource management stories also cross this territory. The DOC pamphlet *Kā Tiritiri o Te Moana* covers this cultural dimension.

reviews.summary: Visitors who engage with the Kāi Tahu cultural landscape of the Whakatipu basin describe it as transforming their understanding of the view — the mountains and lake become living tradition rather than backdrop. Reviewers recommend reading Ngāi Tahu's own materials before arriving. The heartbeat lake legend is consistently described as the most memorable piece of knowledge people take home from Queenstown.

pois.name: Te Ao Mārama — Ngāi Tahu Tourism Cultural Experience, Queenstown
pois.short: Ngāi Tahu's own guided cultural experience in Queenstown — story, song, whakapapa, and the Kāi Tahu relationship with the Whakatipu landscape.
pois.commentary: Ngāi Tahu Tourism (the commercial arm of the Ngāi Tahu tribal authority, which is NZ's largest iwi by land area) operates a growing suite of culturally grounded experiences. In the Queenstown district this includes guided narrative experiences centred on the Whakatipu basin, connecting the landscape features visible from town to the oral traditions and whakapapa of the local hapū. These are guided, small-group experiences led by Ngāi Tahu members. Experiences and booking points change seasonally — the Queenstown i-SITE carries current information, and the Ngāi Tahu Tourism website lists all active products. This is the culturally authorised way to access Kāi Tahu knowledge of this landscape.

reviews.summary: Visitors who take a Ngāi Tahu-led cultural experience in the Queenstown area describe it as one of the most meaningful activities they did in New Zealand — hearing the landscape explained by its traditional owners gives every subsequent vista a different dimension. Reviewers note the guides are passionate and knowledgeable in ways no guidebook can replicate.

pois.name: Tāhuna — Queenstown's Māori Name & Te Ara o Tāhuna Walk
pois.short: The waterfront walk that traces the Kāi Tahu meaning of Tāhuna — 'the shallow bay' — through bilingual interpretation panels.
pois.commentary: Tāhuna — Queenstown's Kāi Tahu name — means 'the shallow bay or sandbank'. The lakefront esplanade between the Queenstown Bay beach and the Queenstown Gardens peninsula has been progressively interpreted with bilingual signage developed in partnership with Ngāi Tahu, naming and explaining the Kāi Tahu origins of major landscape features visible from the waterfront: the lake, the Remarkables range, the distant Pisa Range, and the outlet at the southern end where the Kawarau River begins. The 20-minute waterfront walk is free. Most visitors focus on the TSS Earnslaw — the signage is subtle and rewards those who slow down enough to read it.

reviews.summary: Visitors who notice the bilingual waterfront panels describe them as one of the most quietly effective pieces of public cultural interpretation in the Queenstown area. Reviewers note it is possible to walk the entire waterfront without registering the Māori language content — which they recommend actively seeking out. Pairs naturally with any Ngāi Tahu guided experience.

pois.name: The Sherwood Hotel & Dining, Queenstown
pois.short: Queenstown's most creatively-minded hotel restaurant — a sustainably-run kitchen focused on South Island provenance and seasonal menus.
pois.commentary: The Sherwood is a converted 1960s motel on Frankton Road, intentionally redesigned as Queenstown's 'slow tourism' alternative — sustainability-certified, with a kitchen garden, a resident beekeeper, and a restaurant that changes its menu weekly based on what's available from named Central Otago and Southland suppliers. The food is genuinely creative without being pretentious — Central Otago pinot reductions, South Island salmon, Southland venison. The hotel's bar doubles as a live music venue, hosting Queenstown's most eclectic programme of local and touring musicians. A deliberate counter-position to the adventure-tourism mainstream.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe The Sherwood as the best kept secret in Queenstown's food scene — genuinely creative cooking, an excellent Central Otago wine list, and an atmosphere that prioritises local culture over tourist throughput. The changing seasonal menu is consistently praised. Reviewers recommend booking the restaurant independently of hotel accommodation.

pois.name: Wakatipu Brewing Company, Queenstown
pois.short: Queenstown's own craft brewery taproom — local water, Central Otago hops, and mountain views from the beer garden.
pois.commentary: Wakatipu Brewing Company opened its Queenstown taproom in 2015 and has become one of the town's genuine local-favourite drinking spots — a counterpoint to the Irish pubs and international-brand bars on Beach Street. The brewery emphasises New Zealand hop varieties (especially Motueka and Nelson Sauvin) and uses Whakatipu water, which is notably mineral-clean. Year-round taps include the flagship Pale Ale and a rotating seasonal programme. The beer garden has views toward the Remarkables. The brewery also supplies most of the better Queenstown restaurants — asking for Wakatipu on tap is a reasonable test of a restaurant's local commitment.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Wakatipu taproom as one of the most authentically local spots in Queenstown's otherwise heavily-touristed food and drink scene. The Pale Ale is consistently called the best beer in the region. Reviewers recommend the beer garden on a summer evening as the most relaxed version of Queenstown.

pois.name: Elspeth & Neil's — Arrowtown Fine Dining
pois.short: Arrowtown's most intimate fine-dining room — a seasonal menu driven by Central Otago provenance, matched to the region's best pinot noir.
pois.commentary: The Arrowtown dining scene around Buckingham Street punches above its weight: the village's gold-rush calm, the proximity to Queenstown, and the Central Otago wine country immediately around it have attracted a concentration of small, serious restaurants. The most praised for local produce and wine matching is the intimate room on Buckingham Street — a seasonal menu of perhaps six dishes that changes frequently and leans into Central Otago stone fruit, Queenstown Lakes lamb, and local game. The wine list is the best showcase of the Bannockburn, Gibbston, and Cromwell Basin sub-regions available in Arrowtown.

reviews.summary: Diners consistently call this Arrowtown's most memorable restaurant experience — the combination of intimate scale, changing seasonal menu, and a wine list that reads as a tour of the Central Otago cellar doors makes it a destination in itself. Reviewers recommend booking well in advance. The pinot noir matching is described as one of the best wine-pairing experiences in the South Island.

pois.name: Shotover Jet
pois.short: Iconic jet boat ride through the narrow Shotover River canyons at 85km/h.
pois.commentary: Shotover Jet is Queenstown's original jet boat experience, blasting through the narrow Shotover River canyons at speeds up to 85 kilometres per hour just centimetres from the rock walls. The Big Red jet boats have been operating since 1965. The 25-minute ride includes heart-stopping 360-degree spins in water just 10 centimetres deep.

reviews.summary: An absolute rush. The canyon scenery adds drama to the speed. An essential Queenstown experience.

pois.name: Arrowtown Autumn Festival / Heritage Village
pois.short: Charming gold-rush village famous for spectacular autumn colour displays.
pois.commentary: Arrowtown is a beautifully preserved gold-rush village at the foot of the Crown Range, famous across New Zealand for its spectacular autumn colour display. In April and May, the deciduous trees lining the main street and River Walk explode in vivid reds, oranges, and golds against a backdrop of snow-dusted mountains. The Arrowtown Autumn Festival celebrates with markets, music, and community events. Year-round, the heritage main street offers boutique shopping, excellent restaurants, and the Lakes District Museum which covers the gold-rush history and the Chinese miners' settlement. The Arrow River remains a popular recreational gold panning spot. The Chinese Settlement at the edge of town is one of the best-preserved examples in the country.

reviews.summary: Autumn colour here is genuinely spectacular. The heritage village is charming year-round. Excellent restaurants and shopping.

pois.name: Glenorchy & Paradise Valley
pois.short: Gateway to wilderness at the head of Lake Wakatipu, with Lord of the Rings locations.
pois.commentary: Glenorchy sits at the head of Lake Wakatipu, 45 minutes from Queenstown along one of New Zealand's most scenic drives. This tiny settlement is the gateway to some of the country's finest wilderness, including the start of the Routeburn, Greenstone-Caples, and Rees-Dart tracks. Beyond Glenorchy, the road continues to Paradise — a valley so breathtakingly beautiful that Peter Jackson used it extensively for Lord of the Rings filming. The valley's ancient beech forests, braided rivers, and towering peaks create landscapes that genuinely look otherworldly. Horse riding, jet boating, kayaking, and scenic flights operate from Glenorchy. The small town has a cafe, general store, and a handful of accommodation options that prioritise the natural setting.

reviews.summary: The most beautiful valley in NZ — Paradise earns its name. The drive from Queenstown is spectacular. Base for world-class tramping.

pois.name: Ahi Kā Haka & Hāngī Experience
pois.short: Powerful haka performance and traditional hāngī feast in Queenstown.
pois.commentary: Ahi Kā offers Queenstown's most authentic Māori cultural experience — a powerful haka performance followed by a traditional hāngī feast. The evening begins with a pōwhiri (welcome ceremony), followed by demonstrations of Māori arts including poi, weaponry, and the haka — performed with genuine intensity rather than tourist-show polish. The hāngī feast features meat and vegetables slow-cooked in an underground earth oven using hot stones, producing tender, smoky food that's unlike anything else. The guides share genuine cultural knowledge and personal stories, creating an experience that connects visitors emotionally with Māori culture.

reviews.summary: Powerful, authentic cultural experience. The haka is genuinely moving. The hāngī food is delicious. Not a tourist show — the real thing.

pois.name: Central Otago Wine Region
pois.short: World's southernmost wine region famous for outstanding Pinot Noir.
pois.commentary: Central Otago is the world's southernmost major wine region and has earned an international reputation for producing some of the finest Pinot Noir outside Burgundy. The region's extreme continental climate — hot, dry summers and cold winters — combined with its schist soils create wines with intense fruit flavours and elegant structure. Over 100 vineyards are scattered across the dramatic landscape of Bannockburn, Gibbston Valley, Cromwell Basin, and Bendigo. Cellar doors range from intimate boutique operations to architecturally stunning facilities. The combination of exceptional wine, dramatic landscapes, and the goldfields heritage of the surrounding towns makes a Central Otago wine tour one of the South Island's finest experiences.

reviews.summary: World-class Pinot Noir in spectacular scenery. The cellar door experiences are varied and welcoming. Allow a full day.

pois.name: Arrowtown Chinese Settlement
pois.short: Restored gold-rush era Chinese miners' settlement telling a significant immigration story.
pois.commentary: The Arrowtown Chinese Settlement is one of the best-preserved examples of a Chinese gold-mining settlement in New Zealand. During the 1860s gold rush, Chinese miners came to work claims abandoned by European miners, enduring harsh conditions and racial discrimination. The restored settlement includes recreated miners' huts, Ah Lum's Store, and the remains of the settlement's layout. Interpretive panels tell the stories of individual miners and the broader Chinese experience in colonial New Zealand — a chapter of history often overlooked. The settlement is free to visit and sits alongside the Arrow River, where the alluvial gold was won.

reviews.summary: An important and moving heritage site. The individual stories bring history alive. Free and easy to visit alongside Arrowtown.

pois.name: Milford Sound Road Day Trip
pois.short: Full-day scenic drive from Queenstown to Milford Sound through some of NZ's most dramatic scenery.
pois.commentary: The day trip from Queenstown to Milford Sound is one of New Zealand's great road journeys — a full day travelling through landscapes that shift from alpine tussock to mirror-still lakes, ancient beech forest, and glacier-carved valleys before emerging at the iconic fiord. Key stops include the Mirror Lakes, the Eglinton Valley, the Homer Tunnel (a raw, unlined tunnel through solid rock), and the dramatic descent to the fiord. The drive takes about 4 hours each way, with a cruise on Milford Sound in between. Self-driving allows flexibility to stop at viewpoints, while coach tours provide commentary and remove the stress of driving.

reviews.summary: Arguably NZ's greatest road trip. Every section brings new drama. Allow the full day and stop often.

pois.name: Hydro Attack — Shark Submarine
pois.short: Ride a shark-shaped semi-submersible that dives, surfaces, and flies through the air on Lake Wakatipu.
pois.commentary: Hydro Attack is one of Queenstown's most bizarre and exhilarating activities — a semi-submersible shaped like a shark that dives beneath Lake Wakatipu's surface, breaches like a whale, and performs aerial manoeuvres that leave passengers screaming. The custom-built vessel is essentially a cross between a jet ski and a submarine, driven by a professional pilot while the passenger experiences the ride from the front seat. Dives, rolls, and 5-metre jumps out of the water are standard. The whole experience lasts about 25 minutes but packs in more adrenaline than most people can handle. It's genuinely unlike anything else in the world.

reviews.summary: Absolutely insane and utterly unique. Nothing else like it anywhere. Short but intense. Not for the faint-hearted.

pois.name: Gibbston Valley Winery & Wine Cave
pois.short: Central Otago's most famous winery with NZ's largest wine cave carved into schist rock.
pois.commentary: Gibbston Valley Winery is Central Otago's most celebrated winery, featuring New Zealand's largest wine cave — a cathedral-like space carved 76 metres into the schist rock of the Gibbston Valley. The cave maintains a natural temperature of 12-16°C year-round, perfect for storing and aging wine. Cave tours include tastings of their premium Pinot Noir in this dramatic underground setting. The winery's restaurant overlooks the vineyards and valley. The Gibbston Valley itself, known as the 'Valley of Vines', is a dramatic landscape of schist cliffs and sun-drenched terraces where some of the world's finest Pinot Noir is produced. A cycle trail connects the valley's wineries.

reviews.summary: The wine cave is spectacular and the Pinot Noir is outstanding. The restaurant is excellent. A complete wine experience.

pois.name: Dart River Wilderness Jet
pois.short: Jet boat deep into the glacial Dart River wilderness — dramatic mountain scenery and LOTR locations.
pois.commentary: The Dart River Wilderness Jet takes visitors deep into the pristine Dart River valley — a glacially-fed braided river flanked by towering peaks, ancient beech forest, and landscapes used as filming locations for The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit. The jet boat navigates the shallow, braided channels with typical Kiwi skill, reaching areas utterly inaccessible by road. The wilderness setting is awe-inspiring — mountain walls rise thousands of metres on either side, waterfalls cascade from hanging valleys, and the only signs of life are paradise ducks and the occasional deer. The return journey includes a short bush walk through ancient forest. This is jet boating as wilderness exploration rather than pure adrenaline.

reviews.summary: Jet boating through genuinely pristine wilderness. The mountain scenery is overwhelming. LOTR locations add magic. Outstanding guides.

pois.name: Moonlight Track — Mountain Biking
pois.short: Epic backcountry mountain bike ride through gold-mining heritage country above Queenstown.
pois.commentary: The Moonlight Track is one of Queenstown's most rewarding mountain bike experiences — an epic ride through backcountry that was once part of the 1860s gold rush. The track climbs from Arthurs Point through bush and tussock to the historic Moonlight Creek gold workings, with panoramic views of the Shotover River gorge, Coronet Peak, and the Remarkables. The descent back to Moke Lake is thrilling. Old mine workings, water races, and hut ruins punctuate the ride. The track is challenging — fitness and intermediate mountain biking skills are required — but the combination of gold-rush history, alpine scenery, and technical riding is unbeatable. Shuttle services operate from Queenstown.

reviews.summary: Epic backcountry riding with gold-mining heritage. The views are outstanding. Challenging but rewarding. Shuttle-assisted.

pois.name: Lake Hayes Walkway
pois.short: Scenic walk or cycle around a mirror-still lake famous for its mountain reflections.
pois.commentary: Lake Hayes is a small, jewel-like lake between Queenstown and Arrowtown, famous for its mirror-still reflections of the surrounding mountains. A flat, 8km walkway/cycleway circles the entire lake, providing constantly changing views. On calm mornings, the reflections are extraordinary — the Remarkables and Coronet Peak perfectly mirrored in the glassy surface. The walk is flat and easy, suitable for families, and connects to Arrowtown via the Trail of the Queenstown Trail network. Autumn turns the surrounding willows and poplars golden, creating some of the most photographed scenes in the Queenstown area. The lake's modest size means the full circuit takes under 2 hours.

reviews.summary: Mirror-perfect reflections on calm days. Easy flat walking or cycling. Autumn colours are spectacular. Connects nicely to Arrowtown.

pois.name: Bob's Cove Heritage & Wetland Walk
pois.short: A gentle loop through ancient beech forest and wetland on Lake Wakatipu's shore — one of Queenstown's quietest walks.
pois.commentary: Bob's Cove is a 20-minute drive from Queenstown along the Glenorchy road, but it feels a world apart from the adventure capital. The DOC walk loops through red and silver beech forest, across a small wetland boardwalk, and down to a rocky lakeshore with views across Wakatipu to the mountains. Interpretive signs explain the area's history — lime kilns from the 1860s gold-rush era still stand among the trees. The beech forest is atmospheric, with sunlight filtering through the canopy onto a carpet of moss. It's an excellent option for anyone wanting nature without the adrenaline.

reviews.summary: A peaceful contrast to Queenstown's adventure activities. The beech forest is beautiful. Easy walking suitable for all ages. The heritage lime kilns add interest. Can be muddy after rain.

pois.name: Canyon Brewing, Queenstown
pois.short: A craft brewery and restaurant perched above the Shotover River gorge — excellent beer with jaw-dropping canyon views.
pois.commentary: Canyon Brewing sits on the edge of the Shotover River canyon between Queenstown and Arrowtown, with a deck that offers vertigo-inducing views straight down into the gorge. The beer is excellent — a rotating lineup of lagers, ales and seasonal specials brewed on-site — and the food menu is designed to pair with it. The location is the star though; watching jet boats roar through the canyon 100 metres below while sipping a cold IPA is a uniquely Queenstown experience. It's a popular spot so booking is recommended, especially for the outdoor tables.

reviews.summary: The canyon views are spectacular. The beer quality is consistently high. Food is good pub-style fare. Outdoor tables are in high demand — book ahead. A great alternative to the Queenstown bar scene.

pois.name: Skippers Pack Track Heritage Walk
pois.short: Walk the original pack track into Skippers Canyon — the route gold miners took into one of New Zealand's richest goldfields.
pois.commentary: The Skippers Pack Track follows the original route that miners walked into the Shotover River goldfield from the 1860s. The track descends steeply into the canyon, passing the remains of stone buildings, mining equipment and water races. The Shotover was once called 'the richest river in the world' and the ruins along this track tell the story of the thousands who came to seek their fortune. The scenery is dramatic — schist canyon walls, the turquoise river far below, and tussock-covered peaks above. Allow 3 hours return and wear sturdy boots. The history and landscape combine powerfully.

reviews.summary: A fascinating heritage walk with dramatic scenery. The gold-rush history is compelling. Some sections are steep and exposed. Not suitable for very young children. A great alternative to the commercial canyon experiences.

pois.name: Glenorchy to Paradise Road — Film Locations Drive
pois.short: The road from Glenorchy to Paradise through some of New Zealand's most dramatic scenery — a film location for Lord of the Rings and Narnia.
pois.commentary: The road from Glenorchy to the aptly named settlement of Paradise passes through some of the most spectacular scenery in the South Island. Towering beech-clad mountains, braided rivers, and wide glacial valleys provided the backdrop for multiple scenes in The Lord of the Rings (Isengard, Lothlórien, Ithilien) and The Chronicles of Narnia. The road becomes gravel beyond Glenorchy and crosses several shallow fords, so a 4WD or high-clearance vehicle is recommended. Even without the film connections, this landscape is extraordinary — primordial, vast, and humbling. The end of the road at the Dart River is a fitting reward.
