reviews.summary: Jaw-dropping scenery that lives up to its fantasy film heritage. The drive is scenic but the road is rough — take it slowly. A 4WD is recommended. Allow time for stops and photos. Paradise is worth reaching.

pois.name: Wildwire Via Ferrata, Wanaka
pois.short: Climb alongside (and through) waterfalls on iron rungs and cable bridges — the world's highest waterfall via ferrata near Wanaka.
pois.commentary: Wildwire's via ferrata near Wanaka is billed as the world's highest waterfall cable climb, ascending the cliff faces alongside Twin Falls on iron rungs, cable bridges and catwalks fixed into the rock. The experience combines rock climbing, waterfall spray and jaw-dropping exposure in a managed adventure that requires no climbing experience — guides clip you in and talk you up. Multiple levels range from beginner-friendly to genuinely demanding. The Climb & Canyon option adds abseiling and a zipline descent. The combination of vertical adventure, waterfall immersion and alpine scenery makes this one of the most exhilarating activities in the Queenstown-Wanaka region.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary adventure experience. The waterfall setting is spectacular. Guides are excellent and safety-focused. Suitable for beginners on lower levels. The higher levels provide genuine thrills.

pois.name: Dart River FunYak Safari
pois.short: Paddle inflatable kayaks down the crystal-clear Dart River through Lord of the Rings landscapes — accessible wilderness adventure.
pois.commentary: The Dart River FunYak Safari combines a jet-boat ride up the braided Dart River with a gentle paddle downstream in inflatable kayaks (FunYaks). The river flows through ancient beech forest, past towering mountains and through country used as filming locations for The Lord of the Rings. No kayaking experience is needed — the FunYaks are stable and the river is gentle, with guides managing all the logistics. The scenery is jaw-dropping: Tolkien-esque forests, snow-capped peaks reflected in glacial pools, and a profound silence broken only by birdsong and the river's murmur. It's one of the best ways to experience the Glenorchy wilderness without a multi-day tramp.

reviews.summary: An outstanding wilderness experience accessible to all fitness levels. The scenery is breathtaking. Guides are excellent. No kayaking experience needed. Book ahead in summer.

pois.name: 12 Mile Delta Lakeside Walk
pois.short: Peaceful lakeside walk to a secluded beach on Lake Wakatipu.
pois.commentary: While most visitors flock to Queenstown's centre, the 12 Mile Delta walk follows the western shore of Lake Wakatipu to a peaceful stony beach with mountain views. The DOC campsite here is one of the region's best-kept secrets for lakeside camping.

reviews.summary: Blissfully quiet compared to central Queenstown — beautiful lake views.

pois.name: Gibbston Valley Wine Cave
pois.short: Wine tasting in New Zealand's largest underground wine cave.
pois.commentary: Gibbston Valley Winery features New Zealand's largest wine cave, tunnelled 76 metres into the schist hillside where barrels age at constant temperature. Cave tours include tastings of their acclaimed Central Otago Pinot Noir in a dramatic underground setting.

reviews.summary: Stunning wine cave — the pinot noir is excellent.

pois.name: Bob's Cove Track
pois.short: Short bush walk to limestone formations and a hidden lakeside beach.
pois.commentary: Bob's Cove is a peaceful lakeside reserve with a short loop track through beech forest past unusual limestone formations to a secluded rocky beach on Lake Wakatipu. With the mountains reflected in still water, it's a photographer's delight far from the crowds.

reviews.summary: Quiet, beautiful, and only 15 minutes from Queenstown.

pois.name: Moonlight Track Mountain Bike
pois.short: Epic backcountry mountain bike trail following an old gold miners' pack track.
pois.commentary: The Moonlight Track follows an 1860s gold miners' pack route through dramatic schist gorges and tussock high country. The ride descends from the Skipper's Road area through varied terrain — a genuine backcountry adventure combining gold rush history with world-class riding.

reviews.summary: Epic ride through gold rush country — challenging and rewarding.

pois.name: Lake Hayes Circuit Walk
pois.short: Flat circuit around one of New Zealand's most photographed lakes.
pois.commentary: Lake Hayes is famous for its mirror-like reflections of the surrounding mountains. The 8-kilometre circuit track is flat and easy, passing through wetlands and farmland with stunning views. Early morning is best for the signature reflection photographs.

reviews.summary: Stunning reflections — bring your camera early morning.

pois.name: Arrow River Gold Panning
pois.short: Pan for real gold in the Arrow River where the 1860s gold rush began.
pois.commentary: The Arrow River still yields small flakes of gold, and panning is encouraged in designated areas near Arrowtown. It's a hands-on connection to the gold rush history that built the region, and many visitors do find small amounts — a tangible souvenir of Otago's golden past.

reviews.summary: Fun for all ages — you really can find gold!

pois.name: Routeburn Nature Walk — First Section
pois.short: Day walk along the first section of the famous Routeburn Track.
pois.commentary: You don't need to do the whole Great Walk to enjoy the Routeburn. The first section from the shelter to the Routeburn Flats offers magnificent beech forest, the Route Burn river, and mountain views — a taster of one of New Zealand's finest tramping tracks, achievable as a half-day walk.

reviews.summary: Beautiful forest walk — a perfect Great Walk taster.

pois.name: Sam Summers Hut Track
pois.short: A Mt Crichton loop walk to a restored gold prospector's hut, sluiced canyon and creek-side forest.
pois.commentary: Sam Summers Hut Track gives Queenstown a quieter historical counterpoint to its big-ticket thrills. On the Mt Crichton Loop, the track reaches a small schist-and-iron hut built in the 1930s by gold prospector Sam Summers, with nearby sluiced ground and a tailrace tunnel showing how water shaped the old workings. The walk sits beside the Queenstown-Glenorchy Road, so it is easy to add to a day around Lake Wakatipu, yet it feels tucked away in forest and creek country. DOC notes the hut is for day visitors only, not overnight stays.
pois.audio_story: Sam Summers Hut Track gives Queenstown a quieter historical counterpoint to its big-ticket thrills. On the Mt Crichton Loop, the track reaches a small schist-and-iron hut built in the 1930s by gold prospector Sam Summers, with nearby sluiced ground and a tailrace tunnel showing how water shaped the old workings. The walk sits beside the Queenstown-Glenorchy Road, so it is easy to add to a day around Lake Wakatipu, yet it feels tucked away in forest and creek country. DOC notes the hut is for day visitors only, not overnight stays.

reviews.summary: A favourite short Queenstown walk for history, creek scenery and the restored hut; people like its easy access from the Glenorchy road.

pois.name: Mount Crichton Loop Track
pois.short: A forest loop through gold-mining history, creek crossings and the restored Sam Summers hut area.
pois.commentary: Mount Crichton Loop is Queenstown’s quieter goldfield walk. It follows creeks, forest and old workings to a humble hut story, making the region’s mining past feel less like a display and more like a hard life tucked into the gullies.

pois.name: Queenstown Gardens Disc Golf
pois.short: A playful lakeside course weaving through Queenstown Gardens, with views and low-stakes competition.
pois.commentary: Queenstown Gardens disc golf is a rare attraction that is cheap, social and genuinely scenic. The course winds through trees beside the lake, turning a central park stroll into a game without losing the mountain backdrop.
pois.audio_story: Queenstown Gardens disc golf is a rare attraction that is cheap, social and genuinely scenic. The course winds through trees beside the lake, turning a central park stroll into a game without losing the mountain backdrop.

pois.name: Paradise Road, Glenorchy
pois.short: A gravel-edge scenic drive beyond Glenorchy into beech forest, river flats and famous film-location country.
pois.commentary: Paradise Road has a name that sounds like marketing until the valleys open. Beyond Glenorchy the route moves through river flats, beech forest and mountain walls, showing why filmmakers keep coming here and why the road is best driven slowly.

pois.name: Sunshine Bay Track
pois.short: Sunshine Bay Track is a handy lakeside escape from central Queenstown, ending in a quiet bay and small waterfall without demanding a full-day plan.
pois.commentary: Sunshine Bay Track is a handy lakeside escape from central Queenstown, ending in a quiet bay and small waterfall without demanding a full-day plan.

pois.name: Twelve Mile Delta
pois.short: A Lake Wakatipu recreation reserve on the Glenorchy Road, with camping, shoreline walks and mountain views.
pois.commentary: Twelve Mile Delta gives Queenstown a simpler lake edge. The Remarkables and Wakatipu do the visual work, but the place itself is low-key: campsites, shoreline wandering and room to sit with the weather moving across the water.

pois.name: Glenorchy Animal Experience
pois.short: A hands-on Glenorchy farm experience for families, set beneath the head-of-the-lake mountains.
pois.commentary: Glenorchy Animal Experience brings Queenstown’s big mountain scenery down to farm height. It is a friendly, practical stop where children can meet animals and adults can enjoy the surreal contrast between everyday paddocks and the vast scenery beyond them.
